summary: Teams that fail to implement true continuous deployment — where every merge auto-deploys to production in under 15 minutes — pay an enormous hidden cost in engineering headcount and organizational complexity. The delay between writing code and shipping it creates compounding pathologies that force teams to grow far larger than necessary to accomplish the same work.
key_points: True CI/CD means every merge triggers an automatic deploy to production in 15 minutes or less with no human intervention
Deploy delays create exponential headcount costs: a 6-person team with day-long deploys needs 24 people to do the same work
Slow deploys create compounding pathologies: longer diffs, manual processes, batched deploys, blame-hunting
Organizations respond to deploy friction by adding SRE teams, build engineers, managers, and PMs — all of which could be avoided
money_quotes: Most painful of all are the teams who have done all the hard parts — wired up continuous integration, achieved test coverage, etc — but still deploy by hand, thus depriving themselves of the payoff for their hard work.
That's how much your neglect of internal tools and kneejerk fear of autodeploy might be costing you.

The more delay you add into the process of building and shipping code, the more pathologies multiply, and you find yourselves needing to spend more and more time addressing those pathologies instead of making forward progress for the business.
You would be moving more swiftly and confidently as a small, killer team than you ever could at your lumbering size.
key_insight: Deploy delays don't just slow you down linearly — they create compounding organizational pathologies that force exponential growth in headcount and complexity to accomplish the same work a small team with continuous deployment could handle.
